message:
fix: resolve build errors across visualization components

This commit fixes multiple build-blocking issues discovered during
deployment:

hoeffding-article.tsx:
- Replace problematic LaTeX syntax with HTML <em> tags
- The JSX parser was interpreting `{,}` in `$N=5{,}000$` as JavaScript
  expressions, causing syntax errors
- Changed to plain text with <em> for variable emphasis

hoeffding-visualizations.tsx:
- Add pearsonCorrelation() function to replace non-existent d3.corr()
- The d3 library does not export a correlation function; implemented
  standard Pearson correlation formula inline
- Computes: r = Σ(xi-x̄)(yi-ȳ) / √(Σ(xi-x̄)² × Σ(yi-ȳ)²)

app/writing/cmaes_explainer/page.tsx:
- Fix import syntax for SiteHeader and SiteFooter
- Changed from named imports `{ SiteHeader }` to default imports
- Both components use `export default function`

components/barra-article.tsx:
- Add missing TrendingDown import from lucide-react
- Component was used but never imported

components/cmaes-visualizations.tsx:
- Add explicit `number[][]` type annotation to eigendecomposition matrix B
- TypeScript inferred `(0|1)[][]` from identity matrix initialization,
  preventing assignment of floating-point Jacobi rotation results
- Fix bufferAttribute props: use `args={[positions, 3]}` instead of
  separate count/array/itemSize props (R3F API requirement)
- Fix implicit `any` type on nested map callback with explicit
  `val: number` annotation

components/overprompting-visualizations.tsx:
- Remove unused `extend(geometry)` from maath that caused type errors
- The geometry module exports functions, not constructors, making it
  incompatible with R3F's extend() catalogue system
- Remove unused ReactThreeFiber import
- Rename `Line` component to `ConnectionLine` to avoid conflict with
  SVG line element
- Use `<primitive object={new THREE.Line()}>` pattern instead of
  lowercase `<line>` which TypeScript interprets as SVGLineElement

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -281,7 +281,7 @@ export function HoeffdingArticle() { Computational Cost -

Evaluating quadruples ($n \text{ choose } 4$) is expensive. For $N=5{,}000$, we evaluate ~6.2 billion combinations.

+

Evaluating quadruples (n choose 4) is expensive. For N=5,000, we evaluate ~6.2 billion combinations.
